note_data.title: Theory of the firm
keyPoints: The basic economic problem is scarcity - wants are unlimited and resources are finite, so choices have to be made
Opportunity cost is the value of the next best alternative forgone when making a choice
Economic agents like consumers, producers and governments all face opportunity cost decisions due to finite resources

Three key production questions: What to produce (determined by consumer preferences), How to produce it (producers aim to minimize costs), For whom to produce it (those with greatest purchasing power)
Ceteris paribus assumes all other things are held constant or equal when analyzing economic effects
Making decisions at the margin involves considering what the additional cost or benefit will be from the next action
In the short run, at least one factor of production is fixed, limiting response to price changes
In the long run, all factors of production are variable, allowing firms to increase capacity
Positive statements are objective and can be tested with factual evidence - look for words like 'will' and 'is'
Normative statements are subjective, based on value judgements and opinion - look for words like 'should'

definitions.definition: The value of the next best alternative forgone
A key assumption made in economics that assumes all other things are held constant or are equal
Economic theory that suggests each extra unit of a good or service consumed gives the consumer less utility
Objective statements that can be tested with factual evidence, and can consequently be rejected or accepted
Statements based on value judgements that are subjective and based on opinion rather than factual evidence
Period where at least one factor of production is fixed
Period where all factors of production are variable

howToAnswer.instruction: Give one fact in one sentence. No explanation needed.
Give the point + reason using 'because' or 'this is because'
Break down the cause and effect relationship with detailed explanation
Consider both advantages and disadvantages, then reach a supported judgment

modelAnswer.question: Explain why a profit-maximising firm will produce where marginal cost equals marginal revenue. [6 marks]
modelAnswer.answer: A profit-maximising firm produces where MC = MR because this is the output level that generates maximum total profit. If MC < MR, the firm should increase output because the additional revenue from selling one more unit exceeds the additional cost of producing it, thus adding to total profit. If MC > MR, the firm should reduce output because the cost of producing the last unit exceeds the revenue it generates, reducing total profit. Only at MC = MR is profit maximised, as any deviation from this point would reduce total profit. At this equilibrium, the firm cannot increase profit by changing output levels. This principle applies to all profit-maximising firms regardless of market structure.
markBreakdown: Mark 2: If MC < MR, increase output for more profit
Mark 3: If MC > MR, reduce output to avoid losses
Mark 4: Only at MC = MR is profit maximised
Mark 5: No incentive to change output at equilibrium
Mark 6: Applies to all market structures

commonMistakes: Confusing positive and normative statements - remember positive can be tested with evidence while normative are opinion-based
Forgetting that opportunity cost applies to all economic agents, not just consumers
Not understanding that ceteris paribus is essential for isolating the effects of one variable
examinerTips: Look for key words: 'will' and 'is' indicate positive statements, 'should' indicates normative statements
Remember opportunity cost is always the next best alternative, not all alternatives given up
formulas: MR = Change in TR / Change in Q
MC = Change in TC / Change in Q
note_data.keyDiagramDescription: Cost and revenue diagram showing MC, MR, AC, and AR curves with profit-maximising output at MC = MR intersection, plus shaded areas for profit/loss
pastPaperRefs: This topic was tested in M/J 2023 P2 Q4 (8 marks) — they asked students to explain the profit maximization condition and draw a diagram showing short-run equilibrium for a monopolist
This topic appeared in O/N 2022 P2 Q3 (6 marks) — candidates had to analyze the differences between perfect competition and monopolistic competition in terms of efficiency
This topic was examined in M/J 2022 P2 Q5 (10 marks) — students needed to evaluate the advantages and disadvantages of monopolies from both firm and consumer perspectives
This topic featured in O/N 2021 P2 Q2 (4 marks) — they tested understanding of economies and diseconomies of scale with real-world examples

workedExample.question: A firm operating in perfect competition has the following cost structure: Fixed costs = $100, Variable cost per unit = $5. The market price is $8 per unit. Calculate the profit-maximizing output level and total profit when the firm can sell a maximum of 50 units. (6 marks)
steps: Step 1: Identify the profit maximization condition - In perfect competition, firms maximize profit where P = MC
Step 2: Calculate marginal cost - Since variable cost per unit is constant at $5, MC = $5
Step 3: Apply profit maximization rule - P ($8) > MC ($5), so firm should produce maximum possible output = 50 units
Step 4: Calculate total revenue - TR = P × Q = $8 × 50 = $400
Step 5: Calculate total cost - TC = FC + VC = $100 + ($5 × 50) = $350
Step 6: Calculate profit - Profit = TR - TC = $400 - $350 = $50
workedExample.answer: The firm should produce 50 units to maximize profit. At this output level, total profit = $50.
workedExample.markScheme: 2 marks for identifying P=MC condition, 2 marks for correct calculations of TR and TC, 2 marks for final profit calculation

examinerWarnings: Examiner Report 2023: 'Many candidates confused average cost with marginal cost when explaining profit maximization' — always use MC = MR for profit maximization, not AC
Examiner Report 2022: 'Students often drew incorrect diagrams showing loss-making firms still operating' — remember firms only continue production if P > AVC in short run
Examiner Report 2021: 'Candidates frequently mixed up market structures' — clearly distinguish between perfect competition (price taker) and monopoly (price maker) characteristics
note_data.keyDiagram: Essential diagram: Short-run equilibrium graph showing Price/Cost on Y-axis, Quantity on X-axis. Must include: MC curve (U-shaped, rising after minimum), AC curve (U-shaped, above MC initially), MR curve (horizontal line in perfect competition, downward sloping in monopoly), profit-maximizing point where MC intersects MR, shaded rectangle showing profit/loss area between price line and AC curve. Label all curves, axes, equilibrium point (Q*, P*), and profit area clearly.
note_data.quickRecap: Scarcity creates the fundamental economic problem requiring choices between unlimited wants and finite resources. Every choice has an opportunity cost - the value of the next best alternative forgone. Economic analysis uses ceteris paribus to isolate variables and distinguishes between testable positive statements and opinion-based normative statements. Decision-making occurs at the margin considering additional costs and benefits.
